bundles.changelog: First concrete foundation-bundle prerelease package (six foundation-document templates with their own schema and hash baselines).
Reworks the technical-architecture document so cross-cutting content lives in one place and is cross-referenced rather than duplicated.
Your built system can now APPLY foundation-document upgrades (not just detect them); upgrades became repeatable, quieter, and clearer, and merge your edits with the new version where possible.

bundles.recommendation_reason: You now build and run your system one capability at a time, accepting each before the next.
bundles.changelog: You now build and run your system one capability at a time, accepting each before building the next (the build-and-operate loop with per-phase acceptance).

bundles.recommendation_reason: Adds operator orientation and risk-action protections, and lets your system safely apply its own updates.
bundles.changelog: Your system now keeps you oriented and protects risky actions; can receive operating-layer improvements through an upgrade (not just foundation documents); and can check for and safely apply its own updates.

bundles.recommendation_reason: The approval drill now uses an example grounded in what your system actually does.
bundles.changelog: The approval drill (the one-time safety demonstration) now uses an example grounded in what your system actually does, instead of a generic action.

bundles.recommendation_reason: Makes updating your system one safe step and hardens two update-safety details.
bundles.changelog: Updating your system is now one safe step: the assistant refreshes the update tool and applies the update together. Also hardens two safety details — the record of an approved update can no longer be altered, and the start-of-session update check uses the same trusted source as the manual check.

bundles.recommendation_reason: Fixes a safety-check bug that was interrupting your ordinary edits and training you to click through every prompt.
bundles.changelog: Fixes a safety-check bug that was stopping you to approve ordinary edits to your own notes and documents — everyday words like "firm" or "High" were mistaken for risky commands, which both broke auto mode and trained you to click through every prompt. The check now decides by what an action actually does, so it pauses only for genuinely risky, irreversible, or outgoing actions. Also adds a short note explaining why auto mode still pauses before sending, sharing, or deleting.
